question: What is the primary purpose of the HTML <section> element?
options: To create a generic styling container
explanation: The <section> element represents a standalone section of content that is thematically related, such as a chapter, tab, or group of related content.

question: Which element should typically always be present inside a <section>?
explanation: A <section> should always have a heading element (<h1>–<h6>) to describe its purpose and theme.

question: When does a <section> element get an implicit ARIA role of 'region'?
options: Only when it has an accessible name
Never; sections don't have ARIA roles
explanation: A <section> becomes a 'region' landmark only when it has an accessible name, typically via aria-labelledby or aria-label.

question: When should you use <article> instead of <section>?
options: When the content can be independently syndicated or distributed
When the content needs a heading
When you need to nest elements
When the content is part of a larger whole
explanation: Use <article> when content can stand alone and be independently distributed (e.g., in RSS feeds). Use <section> for thematic groupings within a larger document.

question: What is the key difference between <section> and <div>?
explanation: <section> has semantic meaning representing a thematic group. <div> is a generic container with no semantic meaning.

question: Which of the following is a valid use of nested <section> elements?
explanation: Sections can be nested to create hierarchical documents. Each nested section should have its own heading at the appropriate level.

question: What is the default display value of the <section> element?
explanation: Like most semantic HTML5 elements, <section> is a block-level element by default.

question: Which ARIA attribute can give a <section> an accessible name?
explanation: aria-labelledby (referencing a heading's id) or aria-label (inline text) provides an accessible name, converting the section into a region landmark.

question: What is the relationship between <section> and the HTML5 document outline?
options: <section> creates a new entry in the document outline
Only <section> with IDs create outline entries
explanation: The HTML5 outline algorithm defines that <section> elements create new entries in the document outline, though this algorithm is not widely implemented.

question: Can a <section> contain an <article>?
options: Yes, an <article> can be inside a <section>
Only if the section has an ID
explanation: A <section> can contain an <article>, and vice versa. This is common when a section groups related articles.

question: When should you NOT use a <section> element?
options: For purely stylistic wrapping with no thematic relationship
explanation: Use <div> instead of <section> when you only need a container for styling or scripting purposes with no thematic relationship.

question: What happens if you use <section> without a heading?
options: The HTML validator will show a warning
explanation: While not strictly invalid, using <section> without a heading is considered a best practice violation and the HTML validator may show a warning.

question: Which element can contain a <section>?
explanation: A <section> can be contained within <main>, <article>, <section> (nested), <aside>, and other flow content containers.

question: How should you provide an accessible name to a <section>?
explanation: Use aria-labelledby to reference the id of a heading element, or aria-label to provide an inline accessible name.

question: What is the difference between <section> and <main>?
options: <main> contains the primary content; <section> groups thematic content
<section> contains the primary content; <main> groups content
explanation: <main> represents the dominant content of the page. <section> represents a thematic grouping within the content. A page has one <main> but can have many <section> elements.

question: Can a <section> be placed directly inside <body>?
explanation: A <section> can be a direct child of <body>, though typically it is nested inside <main>, <article>, or another sectioning element.

question: Which global attributes can be used on a <section>?
explanation: The <section> element supports all global HTML attributes including id, class, style, lang, data-*, aria-*, and more.

question: What is the correct heading hierarchy for nested sections?
options: Use the same level for all nested sections
Headings are not needed in nested sections
explanation: Proper heading hierarchy should use increasing levels (h1 → h2 → h3) as sections are nested deeper, creating a logical document structure.

question: Which situation best calls for a <section> element?
options: A group of three product features on a landing page
A single copyright line in a footer
explanation: A group of product features is a thematically related set of content that belongs in a <section>. Single lines, navigation, and logos belong in their own semantic elements.

question: How do screen readers typically treat a <section> without accessible name?
explanation: Without an accessible name, a <section> does not become a landmark region. Screen readers may still read its contents but won't announce it as a navigable landmark.
